summary: This study examines how deficiencies in one brain connectome modality propagate to the other, using the Krakencoder as a simulation framework. Structural and functional connectomes from 702 healthy participants in the Human Connectome Project were analyzed, with the impact of each of the Yeo-7 functional networks assessed separately. Seven scenarios were considered, each involving the removal of a single network while the remaining networks were preserved. The resulting perturbations in cross-modal predictions were quantified using three complementary metrics: KL divergence on eigenvalue spectra, Frobenius norm, and Wasserstein distance. In addition, the persistence of sex-specific information within the predicted connectomes was evaluated. Across all metrics and both prediction directions, the Default Mode Network produced the largest perturbations, whereas the Somatomotor network yielded the smallest. Sex differences in network-level perturbation signatures were subtle, with the best result being an accuracy of 66.09% from connectomes predicted under network-removal conditions. In contrast, connectomes predicted from intact inputs achieved substantially higher sex classification accuracy, reaching up to 84.76%. These findings confirm that full predicted connectomes retain considerably more sex-discriminative information than perturbation-derived signatures alone.

AI.tldr: Using the Krakencoder framework on 702 HCP participants, the study simulates how deficiencies in brain connectome modalities propagate cross-modally. Removing the Default Mode Network caused the largest perturbations, while Somatomotor removal had the smallest. Full predicted connectomes retained more sex-discriminative information (84.76% accuracy) than perturbation signatures (66.09%).
AI.motivation: To understand how structural or functional connectivity deficiencies in one brain modality affect the other modality, and to explore the persistence of sex-specific information under such perturbations.
AI.method: Analyzed 702 HCP participants' structural and functional connectomes; used Krakencoder for cross-modal prediction; removed each of the Yeo-7 networks individually; quantified perturbations via KL divergence, Frobenius norm, and Wasserstein distance; evaluated sex classification accuracy.
AI.result: Default Mode Network removal caused largest perturbations; Somatomotor removal smallest. Sex classification accuracy: 66.09% from perturbation signatures vs. 84.76% from full predicted connectomes.
AI.conclusion: Full cross-modal predicted connectomes preserve sex-discriminative information much better than perturbation signatures, confirming the robustness of intact connectome predictions for sex classification.

title: Extended Kalman Filter-Based State Estimation for a Nine-Compartment Nonlinear Epidemic Model -- Convergence Analysis and In-Silico Benchmark Calibrated on the COVID-19 Third Wave in Italy
comment: Companion paper to arXiv:2606.07413. Submitted to ISA Transactions. 9 figures
summary: This paper addresses real-time state estimation for a nine-compartment nonlinear COVID-19 epidemic model with two co-circulating strains, a super-spreader subpopulation, vaccination with waning immunity, hospitalization, and mortality. Time-varying transmission and vaccination rates are known inputs from a companion calibration, leaving the reconstruction of all nine states from three routinely reported observables: hospitalizations H, fatalities F, and vaccinated stock V. The contributions are theoretical rather than in the filter recursion. First, a Lie-derivative observability analysis yields, via a six-step derivation, the closed-form determinant |det(O9)| = delta_w * gamma_a^2 * kappa * rho2 * w1^2 * (delta_i - delta_p)^2 * |r1 - r2|, showing the level-2 codistribution is rank-deficient at the calibrated symmetric parameters (delta_i = delta_p, r1 = r2); the third Lie derivative restores full rank 9, with r2 the symmetry-breaking parameter. Second, an EKF is designed on the Euler-discretized dynamics with a closed-form 9x9 Jacobian and Joseph covariance update. Third, local exponential mean-square boundedness of the error is proved as a full theorem via the Reif-Gunther-Yaz-Unbehauen hypotheses, exploiting the bilinear drift and linear output to obtain a global-radius quadratic remainder bound that extends to bilinear-drift, linear-output systems. Fourth, the noise covariances are designed from calibration residuals and assessed by NEES and innovation-whiteness tests. All experiments use synthetic measurements from the calibrated model, so reported RMSE values (0.07%-2.72%) are methodology benchmarks, not predictive accuracy. A parameter-mismatch study shows measured and directly-coupled channels stay accurate under model error up to +/-30% while indirectly observed states degrade gracefully. The framework provides the state-feedback basis for future Model Predictive Control.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theoretical observability framework and Extended Kalman Filter for a nine-compartment nonlinear COVID-19 model, proving rank conditions for observability and establishing error boundedness, with synthetic-data validation showing 0.07%-2.72% RMSE.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time state estimation for a complex COVID-19 epidemic model with two co-circulating strains, super-spreader dynamics, vaccination, and mortality, using only three routinely reported observables (hospitalizations, fatalities, vaccinated stock).
AI.method: Lie-derivative observability analysis yielding closed-form determinant, Extended Kalman Filter with closed-form 9x9 Jacobian and Joseph covariance update, proof of local exponential mean-squared boundedness via Reif-Gunther-Yaz-Unbehauen hypotheses, and noise covariance design from calibration residuals.
AI.result: The level-2 codistribution is rank-deficient at calibrated symmetric parameters (delta_i=delta_p, r1=r2); third Lie derivative restores full rank 9. RMSE values range 0.07%-2.72% on synthetic data. Parameter-mismatch study shows graceful degradation under +/-30% model error.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a state-feedback basis for future Model Predictive Control of COVID-19 epidemics, with proven theoretical guarantees on observability and estimation error boundedness for bilinear-drift, linear-output systems.

title: How Post-Training Shapes Biological Reasoning Models
summary: Scientific reasoning models for biology combine language models with foundation models trained on multimodal biological data, including DNA, RNA, and proteins. These models are built through post-training, yet how each stage shapes reasoning and generalization remains poorly understood. We study when post-training improves performance and when it induces over-specialization. Across genomics, transcriptomics, and proteins, we train and evaluate more than 100 biological reasoning models under controlled variation in backbone, continued pre-training (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL), measuring both in-domain (ID) and out-of-domain (OOD) performance. We find that each post-training stage reshapes generalization in a distinct way rather than contributing uniform gains. CPT improves downstream performance by aligning models with biological language. SFT consistently increases ID performance but causes OOD performance to peak early and decline as models fit the training distribution. RL, when applied to strong SFT checkpoints with aligned rewards, improves OOD performance and partially recovers generalization. These results show that biological reasoning does not improve monotonically with additional supervision or compute. Instead, performance depends on how training stages are composed. Under fixed post-training budgets, the strongest ID-OOD trade-off comes from brief SFT, larger RL allocations, and asymmetric adaptation capacity across stages.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how different post-training stages (CPT, SFT, RL) affect the performance and generalization of biological reasoning models, finding that each stage reshapes generalization differently and that optimal trade-offs come from brief SFT with larger RL allocations.
AI.motivation: To understand how each post-training stage (CPT, SFT, RL) shapes biological reasoning and generalization, and to identify when post-training improves performance versus when it induces over-specialization across genomics, transcriptomics, and proteins domains.
AI.method: Trained and evaluated over 100 biological reasoning models under controlled variation in backbone, continued pre-training (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL), measuring both in-domain (ID) and out-of-domain (OOD) performance.
AI.result: CPT aligns models with biological language and improves downstream performance. SFT increases ID performance but causes OOD performance to peak early and decline. RL on strong SFT checkpoints improves OOD performance and partially recovers generalization. The strongest ID-OOD trade-off comes from brief SFT, larger RL allocations, and asymmetric adaptation capacity across stages.
AI.conclusion: Biological reasoning does not improve monotonically with additional supervision or compute; performance depends on how training stages are composed rather than simply scaling up.

title: Infant Spontaneous Movement Noise Improves Exploration in Deep RL
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. Accepted at IEEE ICDL 2026. Cite as: F. M. López, M. R. Ernst, F. Cruz, M. Hoffmann, and J. Triesch, "Infant Spontaneous Movement Noise Improves Exploration in Deep RL", in 2026 IEEE International Conference on Development and Learning (ICDL). IEEE, 2026, pp. 1-6
summary: Exploration in deep reinforcement learning (RL) is commonly implemented as temporally uncorrelated white noise. However, recent works show that temporally correlated colored noise can improve exploration efficiency by producing smooth trajectories with better coverage of the state space. We inquire whether action noise inspired by infant spontaneous movements can also improve exploration in deep RL. We find that the power spectral densities of babies' end-effector velocities follow a colored noise process where the spectral exponent increases with age. Inspired by this developmental pattern, we introduce a mechanism that progressively increases the temporal auto-correlation of exploration noise during RL training, matching the infant statistics. Experiments across several RL environments show that infant-inspired noise produces structured exploratory behavior and can improve learning efficiency compared to conventional exploration strategies. These findings suggest that human motor and cognitive development can provide useful guidance for designing learning mechanisms in artificial agents. Our code is available at https://github.com/trieschlab/baby-noise-rl.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces exploration noise inspired by infant spontaneous movements, where the spectral exponent of babies' end-effector velocities increases with age. The authors propose a mechanism that progressively increases temporal auto-correlation of exploration noise during RL training, matching infant statistics, and show it improves learning efficiency across several RL environments.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether action noise inspired by infant spontaneous movements can improve exploration in deep reinforcement learning, drawing inspiration from human motor development.
AI.method: The authors analyze the power spectral densities of babies' end-effector velocities, finding they follow a colored noise process with age-increasing spectral exponent. They implement a mechanism that progressively increases temporal auto-correlation of exploration noise during RL training to match these infant statistics.
AI.result: Infant-inspired noise produces structured exploratory behavior and improves learning efficiency compared to conventional exploration strategies (e.g., white noise) across several RL environments.
AI.conclusion: Human motor and cognitive development can provide useful guidance for designing learning mechanisms in artificial agents.

title: Oscillating concentrations suppress condensate coarsening
summary: Living cells utilize condensates to spatially concentrate molecules in response to dynamic signals. For instance, nuclear condensates respond to oscillations in transcription factor levels in the nucleoplasm, including those involved in repairing multiple DNA breaks. To understand how oscillating signals affect condensates, we analyze a theoretical model using numerical simulations and analytical theory. While passive dynamics would drive all molecules into a single condensate, we find that sufficiently fast oscillations stabilize multiple droplets, allowing control of their sizes. We thus reveal a new behavior of chemically active droplets, which could be exploited in synthetic applications.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that fast oscillations in transcription factor levels can stabilize multiple condensates, preventing coalescence into a single droplet, revealing a new behavior of chemically active droplets.
AI.motivation: Living cells use condensates to concentrate molecules in response to dynamic signals like oscillating transcription factors, but how oscillating signals affect condensate behavior is not well understood.
AI.method: The authors analyze a theoretical model using numerical simulations and analytical theory.
AI.result: Sufficiently fast oscillations stabilize multiple droplets and allow control of their sizes, whereas passive dynamics would drive all molecules into a single condensate.
AI.conclusion: This reveals a new behavior of chemically active droplets that could be exploited in synthetic applications.

title: Adaptive inference and function vectors in deep transformers
summary: Transformers are widely used as a general-purpose substrate for learning complex correlations between a large collection of coupled variables, but their internal mechanisms have remained mysterious. We introduce a theory of a deep transformer as a mean-field interacting system that implements distributed inference, subject to constraints on communication, locality and depth. We show that such a system can exploit internal state representations ('function vectors') to infer a latent context variable at increasingly finer scales over its layers. In an in-context regression task, the theory predicts a non-trivial relationship between non-Gaussian, hierarchical structure in the latent context variable, and transformer depth. Predictions are tested using constrained linear attention transformers and demonstrate adaptive inference in deep architectures. Feedforward blocks and depth enable transformers to implement a much richer class of in-context learning algorithms than previously described.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a theory of deep transformers as mean-field interacting systems that perform distributed inference, using internal 'function vectors' to infer latent context variables across layers, with predictions tested on constrained linear attention models showing adaptive in-context learning.
AI.motivation: Transformers are widely used for learning complex correlations between coupled variables, yet their internal mechanisms remain poorly understood. The authors aim to provide a theoretical framework explaining how transformers process information across layers.
AI.method: The authors propose a theory of deep transformers as mean-field interacting systems with constraints on communication, locality, and depth. They model internal state representations ('function vectors') that infer latent context variables at increasingly finer scales. Predictions are tested using constrained linear attention transformers.
AI.result: The theory predicts a non-trivial relationship between non-Gaussian, hierarchical structure in latent context variables and transformer depth. Experiments on in-context regression tasks confirm adaptive inference in deep architectures. Feedforward blocks and depth enable richer in-context learning algorithms than previously described.
AI.conclusion: Deep transformers can exploit hierarchical internal representations for adaptive inference, and the combination of feedforward blocks with depth significantly expands the range of in-context learning capabilities beyond prior understanding.

title: Latent space mapping of interpretable structural coordinates from stochastic single-molecule signals
summary: Nanopores are versatile single-molecular sensors, but their utility is fundamentally constrained by stochastic translocation dynamics warping any encoded information. We resolve it by shifting from time-domain analysis to a learned latent-space mapping via a contrastive encoder trained exclusively on simulated signals from a physics-informed model. This encoder maps solid-state nanopore signals of engineered DNA barcodes into an interpretable molecular coordinate system. The learned representation is responsive to structural barcode parameters while remaining invariant to acquisition conditions and translocation conformation, allowing data pooling across devices. Molecule identification requires a single pass through the encoder, reducing computational cost by three orders of magnitude relative to alignment-based methods. We experimentally validate through mixture quantification, rare-variant detection, consensus barcode reconstruction, and real-time signal acquisition. This shift from temporal analysis to mapping structural coordinates into a latent space changes the paradigm behind analyzing stochastic sensor signals by linking classification to interpretable encoded molecular information.

AI.tldr: A contrastive encoder trained on physics-informed simulations maps solid-state nanopore signals into an interpretable latent space, enabling efficient, accurate DNA barcode identification invariant to translocation conditions and three orders of magnitude faster than alignment-based methods.
AI.motivation: Nanopore sensors suffer from stochastic translocation dynamics that distort signals in the time domain, fundamentally limiting their utility for information encoding and molecule identification.
AI.method: Shift from time-domain analysis to latent-space mapping using a contrastive encoder trained exclusively on simulated signals from a physics-informed model.
AI.result: The learned representation is responsive to structural barcode parameters while invariant to acquisition conditions and conformation, enabling data pooling across devices. Molecule identification requires a single encoder pass, reducing computational cost by three orders of magnitude. Validated on mixture quantification, rare-variant detection, consensus barcode reconstruction, and real-time acquisition.
AI.conclusion: This paradigm shift from temporal analysis to latent-space mapping of structural coordinates fundamentally changes how stochastic sensor signals are analyzed, linking classification directly to interpretable molecular information.
